# Helix Playground Release Notes

Helix Playground Release is a Ping Internal release to support AIC and PingOne teams to build AI Agents for their respective products. Agents help with AI assisted automation whether it is an administrator task to build, configure, troubleshoot, analyze, refine identity use cases or simplify end user experience w/ a chat assisted approach to achieve identity tasks. Though our focus is identity and security, Helix itself is quite generic and can be utilized to build any AI Agents to achieve any tasks.

A developer can get started with Helix using this [URL](https://openam-helix.forgeblocks.com/signup/) (signup).

You can sign up for Helix environment by supplying a Ping email address. You will get an email with information on setting up your credentials. You get admin privileges to the environment, which means you have access to all functions including

* agent builder  
* helix assistant  
* agent testing tools  
* environment configuration include IDP settings  
* access to the data lake  
* access to vector store

As a **quick start**, you can press the Helix Assistant icon to start interacting with Helix. Underneath the assistant there is a default agent that routes your request to the right large language model to get to the answer. In the Agents menu, you can view the default agent and how it works.

You can reach Helix engineering team at \#helix-playground.

The Helix team will share weekly office hours meeting details in Q1 2025\.

The rest of the document introduces

* Agent basics  
* Helix core capabilities  
* Development  
* Testing  
* CI/CD considerations

# Agent Basics

There are five core concepts to start building Agents

* Generative AI and Large Language Models  
* Prompt Engineering  
* Reasoning and Tools  
* Vector Databases and Retrieval Augmented Generation  
* Conversation memory

## Generative AI and Large Language Models

Generative AI refers to AI systems designed to create new content, such as text, images, audio, or video, by learning patterns from existing data. It can be used for applications like image synthesis, music composition and creating writing. Large Language Models (LLMs) are a specific type of generative AI focused on text-based tasks. Built on transformer architectures and trained on massive datasets, LLMs generate human-like text and perform a wide range of tasks.

Large Language Models (LLMs) are incredibly versatile tools in natural language processing (NLP) due to their ability to perform a wide range of tasks with minimal additional training. For example, LLMs can handle classification tasks, such as categorizing text into predefined classes like spam detection or identifying sentiment (positive, negative, or neutral) in a piece of text. They can also excel in named entity recognition (NER), identifying and labeling specific entities such as names, dates, and locations in a document. While LLMs are not traditional statistical topic modelers, they can infer and describe themes within text or associate content with predefined topics, making them effective for high-level topic modeling.

Beyond analytical tasks, LLMs shine in creative and transformative applications. They are highly capable of content generation, producing coherent and contextually relevant text for use cases like drafting articles, writing stories, or generating marketing copy. They can paraphrase sentences or paragraphs to rephrase content while retaining its original meaning, which is useful for summarization or avoiding redundancy. Additionally, they perform summarization tasks effectively, generating concise overviews of longer pieces of text by distilling key points. This blend of analytical and generative capabilities underscores the adaptability of LLMs across diverse applications in industries ranging from customer support to content creation and data analysis.

Helix supports a set of Generative AI models including LLMs to allow building agents that can process text, images and audio. The table below summarizes the list of models supported in the playground release. 

| Provider | Model | Parameters | Modality | Purpose |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| OpenAI | gpt-4o | N/A | multi-to-text | Text Generation |
| OpenAI | gpt-4o-mini | N/A | multi-to-text | Text Generation |
| OpenAI | whisper-1 | 39M-1.5B | speech-to-text | Speech Recognition |
| OpenAI | tts-1 | N/A | text-to-speech | Speech Generation |
| OpenAI | tts-1-hd | N/A | text-to-speech | Speech Generation |
| OpenAI | dall-e-2 | 3.5B | text-to-image | Image Generation |
| OpenAI | dall-e-3 | N/A | text-to-image | Image Generation |
| Google | gemini-1.5-pro | N/A | multi-to-text | Text Generation |
| Google | gemini-1.5-flash | N/A | multi-to-text | Text Generation |
| Google | gemini-1.5-flash-8b | 8B | multi-to-text | Text Generation |
| Anthropic | claude-3-opus | N/A | multi-to-text | Text Generation |
| Anthropic | claude-3-5-sonnet | N/A | multi-to-text | Text Generation |
| Anthropic | claude-3-haiku | N/A | multi-to-text | Text Generation |

With Anthropic, you will need to bring your own key for now, while we work on a centralized license key.

## Prompt Engineering

Prompt engineering is a method used in artificial intelligence (AI), particularly in natural language processing (NLP), to guide how models like ChatGPT generate responses. It involves carefully crafting the input, or “prompt,” provided to the AI to achieve a desired output. Since these models work by predicting what comes next based on the input, the way you phrase your request can significantly influence the quality, tone, and relevance of the response. For instance, a detailed and specific prompt can result in more accurate and useful answers, while vague prompts might lead to ambiguous or incomplete results. Prompt engineering has become a critical skill for effectively interacting with advanced AI systems, especially in applications like chatbots, content generation, and data analysis.

At its core, prompt engineering is about understanding the strengths and limitations of an AI model and using that knowledge to shape how you interact with it. Techniques include specifying the format of the output, providing context or examples within the prompt, or even explicitly instructing the model to “think step by step.” This process helps the AI better understand the user’s intent and produce results that align closely with their needs.

Few good resources to understand prompt engineering are 

* [https://ai.google.dev/gemini-api/docs/prompting-intro](https://ai.google.dev/gemini-api/docs/prompting-intro)  
* [https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/prompt-engineering](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/prompt-engineering)  
* [https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/prompt-engineering/overview](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/prompt-engineering/overview)

Helix allows choice of LLMs and supports prompt building blocks that include

* system prompt (directive) & prompt template  
* user prompt and prompt template  
* examples  
* output format specification

## Reasoning and Tools {#reasoning-and-tools}

ReACT prompting style is a method used in artificial intelligence (AI) systems to make their reasoning process more transparent and effective. “ReACT” stands for Reasoning and Acting, and it encourages AI models to break their responses into logical steps rather than jumping directly to an answer. This style integrates reasoning (thinking through the problem) with actions (using tools or interacting with the environment). For example, instead of directly answering a question like “What’s the weather in New York?” the AI might reason that it needs to look up the current weather and then use a weather-checking tool to find the answer. By alternating between reasoning and tool usage, ReACT improves the accuracy and explainability of AI outputs.

In practice, ReACT prompting is especially useful when an AI has access to external tools like calculators, databases, or search engines. The prompt guides the AI to think critically about what information it needs, decide on the best tool for the task, and then interpret the tool’s output as part of its response. This structured approach is valuable for complex tasks, such as solving multi-step problems, retrieving up-to-date information, or performing specific actions in dynamic environments. By combining reasoning with action, ReACT prompting enables AI systems to handle tasks more effectively and mimic human-like decision-making processes.

Here is a good resource to read more about ReAct prompting.

* [https://www.promptingguide.ai/techniques/react](https://www.promptingguide.ai/techniques/react)

Helix support few features to greatly simply ReAct programming

* standardized ReAct system template  
* custom Python based tool authoring w/ support for tool manifests  
* support for asynchronous human-in-the-loop for AI assisted actions  
  * async allows extremely large pauses between steps while the agent waits for human to review and provide direction

## Vector Databases and Retrieval Augmented Generation

A vector database is a specialized type of database designed to store and retrieve data represented as high-dimensional vectors. In the context of AI Agent development, data such as text, images, or audio can be converted into numerical vector representations using embedding techniques. These vectors capture the semantic relationships within the data, allowing for advanced similarity searches. For example, a vector database can efficiently identify documents or images most similar to a given query by calculating the distances between their vector representations, often using algorithms like cosine similarity or Euclidean distance. This ability to semantically retrieve information is very useful in building AI Agents.

Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is an AI approach that combines the strengths of information retrieval and generative models to produce highly informed and context-aware outputs. In a RAG setup, an Agent can use information retrieved from a knowledge base (often powered by a vector database) to enhance its responses. When a user query is received, the agent first retrieves relevant data, such as documents or context, from the database based on vector similarity. This retrieved information is then fed into the generative model, ensuring the output is both accurate and contextually enriched. RAG is particularly effective for tasks requiring up-to-date or domain-specific knowledge, such as answering complex questions or generating detailed reports, making it a powerful framework for AI Agent development.

Good resources to understand RAG are below.

* [https://cloud.google.com/use-cases/retrieval-augmented-generation?hl=en](https://cloud.google.com/use-cases/retrieval-augmented-generation?hl=en)  
* [https://help.openai.com/en/articles/8868588-retrieval-augmented-generation-rag-and-semantic-search-for-gpts](https://help.openai.com/en/articles/8868588-retrieval-augmented-generation-rag-and-semantic-search-for-gpts)

## Conversation memory

Conversation memory is integral to the development of AI agents, as it allows them to maintain context and continuity throughout interactions. By retaining information from previous exchanges, an AI can deliver more relevant and coherent responses, thus creating a more natural and human-like interaction. Memory enables the agent to track user preferences, revisit prior topics, and expand upon past discussions; these capabilities are essential for applications such as customer support, personal assistants, and collaborative tools. Sophisticated memory systems can differentiate between short-term context, such as maintaining conversational flow, and long-term knowledge, such as recalling user preferences across multiple interactions. This functionality not only enhances user experience but also increases the efficacy of AI agents in managing complex, multi-step tasks and fostering personalized engagement.

# Helix core capabilities

Helix uses a multi-tenanted ( tenant \= environment) deployment that is built on top of the data platform core layers. It shares the same set of persistence, audit, data pipeline and machine learning  modules that enables a number of products in AIC. We are currently in process of design and development to port the platform to PingOne. Due to its layered architecture, the upper layers are mostly unaffected by various AI and Data providers whether GCP or AWS.

When a developer signs up for Helix, s/he gets an environment that six major sections

* Agent Builder  
* Tools  
* Datastore  
* Data Lake Explorer  
* Admin (Security and User Management)

Each of these sections and the functionality is explained below.

## Login to the environment

Once your account has been created, you can click on the ‘Login With Ping AIC’ button to redirect to the Ping AIC login screen. Please note you can leverage ‘Create an account’ to create more users, but would need Everest team super admins to add you to a tenancy with a specific role.

![][image1]![][image2]

Enter the login details and click Next. You should be able to see environments provisioned for you..

![][image3]

## Agent Builder

Helix provides an interface for building AI Agents without requiring extensive programming ability. If you’re familiar with graphing/flow software, this interface should feel somewhat familiar.

The flow can be considered as a DAG (directed acrylic graph). You can choose to have as many inputs as you like. You can feed these inputs to an AI task, function, decision or vector search step. You can feed the output of any step fed to other steps. There is no hard limit on the total number of steps. You can sequence these steps anyway you like including a combination of parallel and serial steps. You can have the agent complete its work and respond back with one or more step outputs to the client. 

Helix, like other flow builders, uses a canvas with a set of nodes to represent inputs and each step. Authors of the Agents drag and drop nodes to a canvas to build the agent and link them together, directing their order of execution.

Below, we’ve created a new draft Agent named Demo01. On the left-hand side of our workspace, we can see the nodes available to us. At the top, there are controls to zoom in/out of our workspace, delete nodes in the workspace, as well as the ability to Save, Debug, and Run our Agent. The Demo01 agent below takes two inputs, text and file and feeds that input to an AI task. The output of the AI task is returned back to the caller. 

Helix playground release supports the following node types

* Text Input  
* File Input  
* AI Task  
* Function  
* Vector Search  
* AI Decision  
* Function Decision

We will cover each of these nodes below.  
![][image4]

### File Input {#file-input}

![][image5]

The File Input node allows for the loading of [files stored](#data-lake-explorer) in the Data Lake. These files may be used as inputs into your Agent. Supported formats include pdf, csv, txt, jpg, jpeg, png, mp3, mp4, wav, m4a.

When you drag a File Input onto the workspace and select it, you see a new panel along the right side of the screen. The panel allows you to change name, add additional ‘outcome’ points if we want to pass this file to multiple successor nodes, and select which file in the Datalake to use as the source.  
**![][image6]**

The select file button allows you to choose files from the data lake, in addition to creating new files as a convenience for completing the file selection. 

![][image7]

### Text Input

![][image8]

Text Inputs provide a way to feed text into your Agent. This could be a static text fed to other nodes as a side-input OR a piece of text sent by the client to the Agent runtime  

As an example, a typical chat application may receive input from a user and call an Agent. The text received from the user could be passed in the request to the Agent. This text is then consumed by the Agent at runtime.

Like the **File Input** node, **Text Input** configuration allows you to rename the node, provide text using the **Edit Text Input** button, and add additional outcomes for feeding to multiple successive nodes.

![][image9]

![][image10]

### AI Task

![][image11]

AI Task is the central node in Helix, where you can perform AI assisted tasks using Generative AI/LLM models. The key to unlock value is to apply the five concepts of task goal (classification, summarization, generation, etc),  prompt engineering, ReAct programming, vector search and conversation memory.

When you drag and drop this node, and open the configuration panel, you see the normal naming and outcome options. But there are a number of important properties here for AI.

![][image12]

### AI Task \- Driver

**Provider** lets you choose the company/foundation that provides or hosts the model we’d like to use in our task. These include OpenAI, Google, and Anthropic.

**Models** lets you choose based on modalities requirements of your agent. Each model falls into a specific category and supports certain modalities. (ie. Text, Multi, etc…) Modality refers to a model’s **Input Type** and **Output Type**. Choosing the right model based on your modality is critical to achieve the AI task. In addition to modality concerns, each model has a different generation speed (ie. latency) and a different cost. As a rule of thumb, we want to choose the cheapest and fastest model that still accomplishes our task successfully.  
![][image13]

As of this writing, OpenAI’s **gpt-4o-mini** is a good starting point if you’re overwhelmed by the options. It is both cheap and fast, while also having the ability to receive multimodal (text/media) inputs and generating a text output.

Below are a few helpful links to guide us in our understanding of different models:  
OpenAI: [https://platform.openai.com/docs/models](https://platform.openai.com/docs/models)  
**Google**: [https://ai.google/discover/our-models](https://ai.google/discover/our-models/)  
**Anthropic**: [https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/about-claude/models](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/about-claude/models?ref=blog.dust.tt)

### AI Task \- Prompt

Correctly building the prompt structure of your **AI Task** is vital to success. Models respond to their inputs, so it’s necessary that we both instruct our model and arrange its received inputs in a way that produces the response we desire.

For text-compatible models (ie. Text-To-Text and Multimodal-To-Text), applying good prompt engineering techniques go a long way. 

#### **AI Task \- Prompt \- System Message**

This is the type of message you use to tell the model “How to behave”, “What to do”, or “How to do it”. It is considered an ‘off-prompt’ message and is not included as part of the conversation. However, the model uses this to dictate its mood, response style/format, and instructions.

It can be helpful to think of the **System Message** as a set of instructions from your boss. We take our boss’s instructions as reasonably authoritative and attempt to perform the task or mode of operation they outline for us. 

For the basic usage of Helix, we’ve exposed a few sections that help a developer build this message.

**Directive**: This is the section where you give the model its operating instructions. In most cases, you tell it what its main objective/purpose is and what role/mood to take in response to its User Message.

![][image14]

**Examples**: Examples act as a sort of ‘scenario’  tutorial for the model. We can outline “If you receive this input” respond with “this output.”

![][image15]

#### **AI Task \- Prompt \- User Message**

The **User Message** can be considered our queries from the user. User messages are part of the conversation’.

Continuing our boss analogy from above, the **User Message** is akin to a message received from a customer or client. We need to respond to this message and we want to fulfill their request, but we should only respond in a way that is in alignment with our boss’ instructions. (ie. **System Message**)

Helix exposes the **User Template** to the developer to arrange the task’s received inputs.

![][image16]

You’ll notice that our **User Template** contains a placeholder of **{{inputCompany123}}**. This is a reference to the task node’s predecessor. We can reference the output of preceding nodes by their ID. This is a common pattern in Helix and can be seen in many areas.

### AI Task \- Input / Output

These sections allow you to choose modality of the inputs and outputs, telling Helix which type of AI Task should be executed. Behind the scenes, there are many different types of AI Tasks – these include Text-To-Text, Text-To-Image, Multimodal-To-Text, Text-To-Speech, and Speech-To-Text. Helix attempts to simplify this by only exposing a single AI Task node.

### Function

![][image17]

**Function** nodes provide developers a flexible and secure way to execute custom Python code within their agent. These functions are executed in a RestrictedEnv that tightly controls the libraries and built-ins the provided function has access to.

Specific to this node type is the function Type (aka. language), as well as a button to edit our script. For now, only Python is supported, but more language support has been planned for future releases.

![][image18]

#### 

If we click the **Edit Script** button, a modal appears that contains a textbox for our new code. Within this box, we will place our function content.

![][image19]

Some guidelines

* Function arguments should be the ID of preceding nodes. This gives your Function access to the output of its predecessors.  
* Function arguments MUST always be typed. This can be achieved using the standard :\<type\> syntax that is common to Python.

Available Libraries include  \["typing","jose","sklearn","random", "jwt","re","time","uuid","ssl", "numpy", "tiktoken","base64", "json", "math", "datetime", "yaml", "matplotlib", "boto3", "pandas", "bs4", "types",  "certifi", "pydantic", "enum", "asyncio",  "requests", "io", "aiohttp",  "\_strptime"\]

### AI Decision

AI Decisions act as a ‘Natural Language’ controller or router. This allows the developer to conditionally execute subsequent nodes in the Agent, based upon provided ‘Natural Language’ conditions. A Provider and Model are required to act as the ‘Decision Maker’ for the node and control the routing of the execution.

![][image20]

![][image21]

An **Outcome** section allows you to associate an outcome with a **condition**. The **condition** is expected to be a natural language description of how the model should choose the **outcome**. In a typical **decision**, there will be more than one option. We can add an additional option by clicking the **Add Outcome** button. The Agent can take multiple outcomes, suggesting that each chosen path is evaluated for the purpose for execution.

Below, is a sport example of using natural language conditions to choose the outcome and the path taken.  
![][image22]

If we link a Text Input and a couple of new AI Tasks to this Decision node, we can see that we now have a simple example of how this node type can be used. In this trivial sample, we can pass an NFL Team as the input and Debug our Agent. We see that the Decision node chooses its NFL option/outcome  to continue its execution. All options/outcomes that are not chosen will be immediately stopped.

![][image23]

### Function Decision

Function Decisions act as a logic based router. This allows the developer to conditionally execute subsequent nodes in the Agent, based upon provided Script that evaluates and determines the path..   
![][image24]

You can add outcomes and determine those outcomes based on the script that you provide by pressing the Edit Script button. 

Below is an example of a Function Decision node that has three possible paths that are determined by the Python code provided in the node. Unlike the AI Decision node, there is no AI model evaluation involved. Rather you determine the route programmatically.

As with AI Decision, you can choose multiple outcomes, suggesting that each chosen path is evaluated for the purpose for execution.

![][image25]

### VectorSearch {#vectorsearch}

**VectorSearch** is the retrieval node in the Helix platform. A developer may provide a natural language query with table name to search against the datastore. 

![][image26]

After dropping a Vector Search node into your workspace, you have the option to enter the table name that you created in [Datastore](#datastore-entities) to store the embeddings. You will have the option to enter the search string as well.

![][image27]

### Output Node

**Output Node** is a static node added by default to the workspace. There is no right panel configuration enabled for the output node. It is MANDATORY to connect at least one of the previous nodes to the output node as the engine executes till all requirements for the output node are met.

![][image28]

## 

## Memory Configuration \- Task Node / AI Decision Node

### Overview

Helix provides developers with the ability to configure **memory** for `Task` and `AI Decision` nodes to enable better context-aware interactions with LLMs. By default, memory is **disabled** for these nodes to optimize performance and avoid unnecessary context bloat. However, when memory is needed for deeper understanding or continuity across the workflow, developers can enable and manage memory through a simple interface.

---

### What Is Memory in Helix?

Memory in Helix refers to the ability of `Task` and `AI Decision` nodes to access previous interactions or node outputs. This historical context is passed to the LLM so it can make more informed decisions or generate more accurate responses.

---

### Default Behavior

* **Memory is OFF by default** for:

  * `Task` Nodes

  * `AI Decision` Nodes

* This ensures that nodes only process the immediate prompt unless memory is explicitly configured.

---

### How to Enable and Manage Memory

To configure memory for a `Task` or `AI Decision` node:

1. **Select the Node**  
    Click on the `Task` or `AI Decision` node you want to configure in the workflow DAG.

2. **Click "Manage Memory"**  
    You’ll see a button labeled **Manage Memory** in the node configuration panel.

3. **Choose Memory Sources**  
    A dialog will appear where you can:

   * Select **Prompt (input)** and/or **Complete (output)** values

   * From:

     * Input Nodes (Text Input, File Input)

     * Other Task or AI Decision Nodes

     * End Node

     * **Self** (the current node’s previous runs, if applicable)

4. **Save the Configuration**  
    Once selections are made, save the memory configuration. Memory is now enabled and tailored for that specific node.

![][image29]

---

### 

### How Memory Is Used

When memory is enabled:

* The selected node (Task or AI Decision) will automatically **include a chronological list of past messages** from the selected sources.

* This list is passed as part of the **LLM context**, allowing the model to:

  * Maintain conversation continuity

  * Reference previous outputs

  * Make more informed decisions based on past interactions

---

## 

### Use Cases

Here are some practical examples of when to enable memory:

* **Multi-turn conversations:** Keep context for a chatbot across multiple user messages.

* **Decision branching:** Allow AI to choose different paths based on earlier answers or conditions.

* **Personalization:** Use previous inputs or user messages to tailor LLM responses.

---

### Best Practices

* **Use memory selectively** to avoid performance issues with large context windows.

* **Be mindful of the token limits** of the selected model.

* **Organize nodes logically** so memory configurations stay intuitive and maintainable.

* **Review memory selections** during debugging to ensure correct context is being passed.

---

### Example

Let's say you have this flow:

1. **Text Input Node** – User provides question

2. **Task Node A** – Calls LLM for answer

3. **AI Decision Node** – Routes based on answer

4. **Task Node B** – Performs follow-up task

To make Task Node B aware of the original user input and LLM’s first response, enable memory on Task Node B and select:

* Complete of Text Input Node

* Complete of Task Node A

This ensures Task Node B can "remember" the conversation so far.

## Templating

Within Helix Studio, each node has several configuration options, many of which are presented as forms. These forms help developers build and tailor the execution of the node according to their needs. For a **Function** node, you may enter your function into the form. For **AI Task** nodes, a developer has access to various prompt-related forms. (ie. *Directive*, *Examples*, etc…)

While hinted at in previous sections, it is less well-known that each of these forms also acts as a template. When a node begins execution, these templates are rendered via the standard Jinja templating engine. This means that you may utilize many powerful templating features when deciding how your node should operate. By default, each node has access to all of its received **inputs** via the preceding node identifiers.

### Placeholder Example (Task)

If we take a simple example using an **AI Task**’s *UserTemplate*, we can precisely format how the task/model receives the user message. Below, we have a small agent that receives a ***username*** and the user’s ***location***. From these, we do nothing more than produce a greeting.

First, we’ll build the general structure of our agent. We have our two inputs, a task node, and the final output node.

![][image30]

Behind the scenes, I’ve also given the task a generic *Directive* to produce a greeting based on the user’s ***username*** and ***location***.

Next, let’s modify the *UserTemplate*. We want to format the received ***username*** and ***location*** text inputs to always be presented to the task in a more structured manner. We note that we’re receiving user information, as well as the aforementioned labeled inputs.

Notice the **{{ }}** syntax. This is the standard ‘direct-access’ way to introduce received inputs into your template. Inside the **{{ }}** we’ve placed the node identifiers of our respective text inputs.

![][image31]

With this new *UserTemplate*, we can now save the agent and perform a quick run.

![][image32]

Analyzing the output trace for our ***Greeting Task***, we can see that the user message contains the \*\*formatted user message.

*\*\*The displayed format found in the Studio UI differs slightly from how the task received our template. This will be fixed in future iterations.*

### Advanced Examples

#### Conditionals

Imagine we have an agent that generates a message for a student, producing a simple assessment about how well they performed on their test. This agent accepts the student’s ***name*** and ***score***. The agent also accepts several sample statements/responses we’d like to give the **AI Task** as examples.

Our desire is that the model will use these examples to generate its own message. In fact, by including the direct input in the agent, we can tailor these very examples to the current agent interaction with the ***user***.

Below, we see that we’re using the ***score*** represented in ***function52f32d765d2f*** to compare against some arbitrary thresholds of 25%, 50%, and 75%. The greeting is formed using the student ***name*** found in ***textInput7d1ee37427d9***. The sample responses/statements all exist within the conditionals assessing the test ***score***.  
![][image33]

**\[Will expand section as user needs dictate\]**

## Triggers

### Overview

In Helix, AI Agents are designed using **Agent flows**, which consist of various connected nodes representing inputs, decisions, logic, and AI tasks. When using branching logic—such as with **AI Decision** or **Function Decision** nodes—downstream nodes may receive inputs from multiple sources, including some that were logically abandoned.

To manage execution in these scenarios, Helix introduces **Trigger Rules**, allowing developers to define precise conditions for when a node should execute based on upstream inputs.

---

### 

### Default Trigger Behavior

Without custom rules, Helix uses a built-in trigger mechanism that ensures a node is executed when inputs from both decision and non-decision sources are received:

```
trigger:
  and:
    - or:
        - decision01
        - ...
        - decision<N>
    - or:
        - regularInput01
        - ...
        - regularInput<N>
```

### 

### New Feature: Visual Trigger Rule Builder

As of this release, Helix Studio includes a **visual Trigger Rule Builder**. This feature gives developers the ability to configure when a node should execute based on specific combinations of upstream inputs — all through an intuitive UI.

---

### 

### 

### 

### How It Works

#### Steps to Configure Trigger Rules:

1. Open your **Agent flow** in Helix Studio.

2. Select a node that receives input from multiple sources.

3. In the configuration panel, find the **"Manage Triggers"** section.

4. Use the **Trigger Rule Builder** to set:

   * **Trigger logic**: Any or All

   * **Input properties**: Define the exact sources that must be active

### Use Case Examples

#### **1\.** Combined Input Requirement

If a node depends on all the inputs, set the trigger rule to **All** so it only runs when all inputs are available.

#### 2\. First-Available Execution

For scenarios where the node can execute when all predecessor nodes are complete/stopped/etc, use the **Any** condition.

#### 3\. Avoiding Unintended Execution

If a node should **only** trigger under very specific circumstances, define those explicitly using the **Select Property** fields and use **All** for strict matching.

![][image34]

### Best Practices

* Use **All** when synchronization between branches is essential.

* Use **Any** to allow early, parallel, or fail-safe executions.

* Customize triggers in complex Agent flows to avoid unwanted behavior from abandoned branches.

* Maintain clear naming on node inputs for readability when setting up trigger rules.

## Node Debugging in Helix

Debugging complex AI workflows can be time-consuming when running entire agents. Helix makes this process easier by providing **node-level debugging**, allowing developers to test and refine individual nodes in isolation.

### Why Node Debug?

Running a complete agent to test small changes can be inefficient. With **Node Debug**, developers can:

* Save time by debugging only the node they are working on.

* Use mock inputs to simulate different conditions.

* Iterate quickly and validate changes incrementally.

---

### How to Debug Different Nodes

Helix supports debugging on several types of nodes via dedicated modals.

#### Function Node & Function Decision Node

1. Click **Edit Script** on the desired node.

2. In the script editor modal, toggle the **Debug Mode**.

3. Provide **mock inputs** to simulate data the node would receive during actual runs.

4. Run the script inside the modal to see real-time output.

5. Iterate and tweak logic until you're satisfied with the result.

![][image35]

---

### Benefits of Node-Level Debugging

* Faster development and testing cycles.

* Modular workflow design with easier troubleshooting.

* Better control over logic and template behavior.

---

By leveraging the **Node Debug** feature, developers can build, test, and fine-tune Helix agents with greater speed and confidence.

## Tools

Tools are a powerful way for AI Tasks to accomplish complex AI assisted tasks. One approach that has gained a lot of traction is ReAct (see [Reasoning and Tools](#reasoning-and-tools)). 

Helix supports a number of out-of-box tools.  These include

* Calculator \- Allows ReAct looping to evaluate mathematical expressions  
* CurrentDate \- Allows ReAct looping to get current date  
* GoogleWebSearch \- Allows ReAct looping to perform a google search  
* HumanInput \- Allows ReAct looping to get user input as part of an iteration  
* PythonFunction \- Allows agent authors to create custom Python functions that can be executed to achieve desired outcomes. Unlike other OOTB tools, this is actually a tool type that is used to create concrete tools. The concrete PythonFunction can then be included in the AI Task to allow ReAct looping to invoke the function.  
* PythonScript \- Allows agent authors to create custom Python scripts that can be executed to achieve desired outcomes. Unlike other OOTB tools, this is a tool type, that is used to create concrete tools.  Scripts and functions are very similar in that they allow the author to write Python logic. The main difference is that LLM gets return value for functions but the last expression for the script. The concrete PythonScript can then be included in the AI Task to allow ReAct looping to run the script.  
* WebScrape \- Allows ReAct looping to scrape web content  
* WikipediaSearch \- Allows ReAct looping to perform a Wikipedia specific search

Below is a screen shot of **System Tools** (OOTB tools) included in every tenancy. In addition to system tools, agent authors can go to the **Custom Tools** to create their own PythonFunction or PythonScript based tools. 

![][image36]

Key elements of every tool for use by LLM include

* Name \- that is used to build the prompt sent to LLM  
* Description \- that is used to let LLM understand what the tool performs  
* Parameters \- allowing LLM to suggest the parameters to use for the tool invocation

You can create custom tools using the **Create Tool** button.  
![][image37]

You choose PythonFunction or PythonScript depending on what style of invocation your agent needs and then choose the name and description that you expect to pass to the LLM for reasoning.  
![][image38]  
You can write your logic in the Python editor and list the parameters that the LLM has to fulfill for the purpose of ReAct invocations. 

![][image39]

You can set values for the arguments to your function and also create a list of globals that are available as constants injected into the Python execution runtime.   
![][image40]

Details about how Agents can supply these values as part of overall Agent configuration is described [here](https://openam-helix.forgeblocks.com/dpc#/).

Below is an example of a custom python tool. 

```c
name: humanInput
type: humanInput
manifest:
  description: This python function retrieves the user list
  parameters: { }
configuration:
  function: |-
    # Auto Generated
    def getUserList():
      import requests
      import json
      """This is an auto generated function task script. Please add types to the parameters."""
      res = requests.get(USER_API)
      response = json.loads(res.text)
      return response
  arguments: {}
  globals:
    USER_API: https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/users
    TOOL_GLOBAL01: TOOL_GLOBAL01
    TOOL_GLOBAL02: TOOL_GLOBAL02
  aliases:
    TOOL_ALIAS01:
      type: oauth
      description: this is generic oauth client for PingOne
    TOOL_ALIAS02:
      type: passThrough
      description: Davinci path through approach
    TOOL_ALIAS03:
      type: serviceAccount
```

The configuration object is specific to the PythonFunction and PythonScript type. The key elements include

* function \- represents the python code to execute  
* arguments \- represents the function arguments (not to be confused with parameters that are more for the LLM)   
* globals \- represents variables that can be set by the agent context, so the author can avoid hardcoding. For instance, a variable such as USER\_API could vary between dev/stage/prod environments. Instead of hard-coding the value in the PythonFunction, the author could leverage globals. Globals are visible to the author via Agent API and can be set as part of Agent import. When Agent involves the tool, it passes the Agent specific value for the tool  
* aliases \- represents security keys that are also not hard-coded in the function. Unlike globals, aliases are used specifically with the get\_security\_headers function to automatically mint the token associated with the alias. The Helix engine retrieves the provisioned credential from the credential store. It is during Agent import / deployment, that the author (during testing time) or SRE (during prod time) can provision these secrets. The data itself is kept in the cloud specific secret manager. For instance in GCP, Helix places them into the Google Secret Manager. 

## 

## Datastore

![][image41]

Datastore gives you the ability to create Tables with dynamic schema. You will have the ability to create a table with a name, give it a description, select an icon for your table and enable the table to store vector embeddings.

![][image42]

Once you have created the table, you will have the ability to add attributes / columns of different datatypes to the table. Each table requires a primary key attribute.  It is recommended to create an ID attribute and mark it as the primary key. You will have the option to auto generate id values.

![][image43]

You will have the ability to mark if a particular attribute stores embeddings. Once you add all the attributes, click on the Green Check mark to update the table definition

![][image44]

### Datastore Entities {#datastore-entities}

Once you have the table and all the attributes defined and saved, you can now create entities or rows to store data in the created table. Click on the ‘View Entities’ button to navigate to the Entities page.

You will have the ability to create new entities or rows for the table defined.

![][image45]

In this example, since ‘Doc Text’ is marked as an embedding. You can perform semantic search on this table. Please note that embeddings are automatically created for the ‘Doc Text’ based on the model selected during the Table Creation.

![][image46]

You will have the ability to perform simple/complex filtering based on semantics and/or actual text itself. 

Datastore integrates directly into AI Agents. Within an AI Agent, if you would like to perform a vector search, you can utilize the [‘Vector Search’](#vectorsearch) Node to perform the action.

## Data Lake Explorer  {#data-lake-explorer}

![][image47]

Data lake explorer gives you the option to manage files and folders in your tenancy. Data lake explorer integrated directly into AI Agents. You can utilize the [‘File Input’](#file-input) node to select files from Data Lake and provide as input to other nodes in the agent.

## Region Support in Helix

### Overview

Helix now supports region-based configuration for tenancies. This prepares the platform for future regulatory compliance by allowing restrictions based on geographic regions.

### Supported Regions

* `us` – United States  
* `eu` – European Union  
* `others` – Rest of the World

### Region in Tenancy Creation

When creating a tenancy via the Helix Admin Key, include the `region` in the payload:

* If the region is omitted or null, it defaults to `us`.

### Existing Tenancies

For tenancies created before region support:

* A **one-time region selection popup** will appear on login if no region is set.  
* The user must select from `us`, `eu`, or `others`.

### Future Use

No restrictions are currently enforced, but region settings will support:

* Limiting model access based on legal availability per region.  
* Ensuring compliance with data residency and regional policies.

### Recommendations

* Always specify the region when creating tenancies.  
* Choose the correct region during the one-time selection for legacy tenants.  
* Design workflows with potential future region-based model access in mind.

## Security  Refer to security Guide: [Helix Security Guide 1.0](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1Ij0kkgnXIInDHdIJVS4Hnmau9aG-1RosxUkEwYAcfEU/edit?tab=t.0)

### Environment Specific IDP

Every environment can be configured to work with its own IDP. For instance a AIC developer can have one Helix environment point to his/her own AIC tenant, while a PingOne developer can have another Helix environment point to a a PingOne environment. 

IDP configuration is accessed from the Admin \> Identity Provider menu. 

![][image48]

### **Agent Identity**

Agents in Helix are identified, authorized and governed.

When an Agent is created, or imported, the platform creates an OAuth Client in the IDP for the Agent. It is then open for standard identity management practices to handle scopes, permissions and authorization for the Agent. 

The user experience is different between AIC and PingOne. AIC supports DCR (Dynamic Client Registration) and Ping One does not. So, OAuth Client registration can be automated in AIC while this registration has to be done manually in PingOne. Helix as an environment is also an OAuth Client (IDP onboarding; not to be confused with Agent Identity itself that is a different OAuth Client). In AIC, the Helix tenant works with the AIC IDP to automatically provision an Agent identity (OAuth Client). In PingOne, the Helix environment admin has to explicitly provide the credential details after using the admin interface in PingOne to create an OAuth client for the Agent.

### **Agent Credential**

Agent Identity is meant to allow OAuth compliant applications to authorize the agent to make API calls. However, not all APIs are OAuth oriented. So, in addition to Agent Identity, Helix allows Agent to possess credentials. The playground release suppors four types of credentials.

* API Key \- Third party apps often use API keys to integrate. Credential API allows registering API keys w/ credential aliases. The aliases can be used by Agents to call the APIs.  
* Service Account \- Some internal APIs in AIC use service accounts to access them. Credential API can be used to provision service accounts, allowing agents to use the service account alias to call the APIs.  
* OAuth \- APIs may need cross domain OAuth use cases, where Helix has to mint the token with an external IDP (not the onboarded tenant IDP). OAuth credential allows Agents to make OAuth based API calls.  
* Passthrough \- The user token is often needed in some PingOne APIs (for example Davinci does not support standard OAuth). 

### **Helix API Keys** 

Published agents can be invoked using API Keys. API Keys are scoped to tenancy and AgentIDs

Only Data Admins can create API keys, Here are the steps

* Login as Data Admin into Everest Data Platform Tenancy, go to the Admin section and create a API Key

![][image49]

![][image50]

* Download/Copy the API Key using Download Option

**![][image51]**

* Use the API key to run promoted Agents. Get the ID of the test1 Agent (your agent)

**![][image52]**

# Developing Agents

There are a number of aspects of Agent development. The key aspects are covered in this section.

## Agent Identifier

The environment ID (previously called helix tenant), the agent name, and the agent version uniquely identify an agent. Within an environment, you can have at most one DRAFT version for a given name and at most one PUBLISHED version. when you publish a draft version, you have to give it a unique version number that does NOT clash with an existing agent version number.

## Agent Input

While the most common case is for Agent to have a single input (usually to accept a prompt from the user on a chatbot), the Agent author has the choice to have more than one input (e.g. text prompt with an accompanying document, image or other artifacts). 

## Agent Debugging

While building an Agent, the author can debug the agent w/o leaving the Agent Canvas. The output panel allows the agent author to see what steps the Agent takes along with useful information about timing, metrics, ReAct steps etc. 

## Helix Assistant

In addition to the Agent canvas based approach above, an agent developer can also use the **Interact** feature to interact with the Agent. From the Agent listing menu, the user can choose the Interact option to open a Helix Assistant.

## **Agent Feedback Feature Documentation**

### **Overview**

The Helix platform provides a built-in feedback mechanism within the chatbot interface, enabling users to easily evaluate and provide feedback on agent responses. This feature is designed to help developers continuously improve their agents based on direct user input.

### **Providing Feedback**

Users interacting with the chatbot can give feedback for any response generated by an agent using the following options:

* **Thumbs Up (👍)**: Indicating that the agent's response met or exceeded expectations.  
* **Thumbs Down (👎)**: Indicating that the agent response did not meet user expectations.  
* **Comments**: Optionally, users can provide detailed textual feedback when selecting either thumbs up or thumbs down, helping developers understand specific aspects of user satisfaction or dissatisfaction.

### **How to Submit Feedback**

1. After the chatbot provides a response, two icons (👍 and 👎) appear below the response.  
2. Select the appropriate icon to rate the response:  
   * Click **Thumbs Up** if satisfied.  
   * Click **Thumbs Down** if not satisfied.  
3. Upon selecting either option, a comment box appears.  
   * Enter additional details or context about the feedback (optional but encouraged).  
4. Click **Submit** to finalize the feedback.  
   

![][image53]

## Evaluation

Unlike regular software applications, agent testing involves a slightly different approach, where the output of the agent keeps changing all the time, but is still acceptable. 

The playground release supports semantic scores. 

Agent Authors can 

* Create test suite from the agent canvas  
* Define a set of test cases for that test suite  
* Run the test suite to get semantic scores  
* Summarize the scores using AUC/ROC and Precision/Recall curves.

# CI/CD for Agents

The Agent teams need control over their Agent Code and test cases. They also want some way to assure that Helix releases can run a minimal set of tests for a specific agent to ensure newer releases work well with existing contracts. 

The CI/CD for Agents is part of a Q1 FRAAS Epic. The two main elements of CI/CD include

* Agent Bundle \- this the packaging of Agent and its tool, config, file and vector store dependencies  
* Security Bundle \- this is the Agent security requirements involving credential provisioning before Agent can fully function

This section will be updated once a more concrete design has been created.

# REST APIs

The Helix transactional & Security APIs can be found [here](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1v2t-BgfmuuOKt6FsHfCYixopcbhZIIx3/view?usp=drive_link).  
The Helix Agent Evaluation APIs can be found [here](https://drive.google.com/open?id=1x2sgSDmbRPd3UKHbhKLFAIRzqPPGyVoD).

# Postman collection

The Postman collection can be found [here](https://drive.google.com/open?id=1GI8j1psHaALlaeuM-4BjZL50yuHedC7-).

# Quick start recordings

Helix Quick start recordings can be found [here](https://drive.google.com/drive/u/0/folders/1j32YtpzCgVG2D0U1G620PSAmmoF1ZvOL).

# Sample Agents for AIC and PingOne environments

You can view a set of demo agents that have details on how to manage credentials to make APIs calls in AIC and PingOne environments. The folder can be found [here](https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1gxUFgc_s7HUFnpTx6bD8RK8y0CRI4X-l).

# FAQ

## Known Issues/ Limitations

* Currently, image generation is limited to less than 1MB size. We are planning to remove this limitation in the near term roadmap. Epic [here](https://pingidentity.atlassian.net/browse/AI-1386).  
* Though there is an API to find Agent’s dependencies such as config, security aliases, tools, files and vector tables, the UI and automation to tell whether they are satisfied is manual effort on part of the author. Future releases will simplify dependency visibility and status. Epic [here](https://pingidentity.atlassian.net/browse/AI-1387).

## How to recover your password? 

In order to recover your password follow next 

* First select Forgort password?

![][image54]

Then add the Username or email address to the account you forgot the password 

![][image55]

Then go to your email account and follow the email instructions.
